teens.explanation: Psalm 67 is missionary because it prays that Israel's blessing will reveal God's way to all nations (Psalm 67:2) — a centripetal mission theology.

teens.question: The opening of Psalm 67:1 echoes what famous OT blessing?
teens.option_a: The Shema from Deuteronomy 6
teens.option_c: The blessing of Jacob in Genesis 49
teens.option_d: The benediction at the end of Leviticus
teens.explanation: Psalm 67:1 echoes the Aaronic blessing: "The LORD make his face shine upon thee, and be gracious unto thee" (Numbers 6:25).

teens.question: How many times does the refrain "let the people praise thee" appear in Psalm 67?
teens.option_a: Once
teens.explanation: The refrain "Let the people praise thee, O God; let all the people praise thee" appears twice — in Psalm 67:3 and 67:5.

adults.question: How does Psalm 67's opening allusion to the Aaronic blessing (Numbers 6:24-26) connect Israel's priestly vocation to missionary purpose?
adults.option_a: Israel is to be a nation that keeps blessings to itself
adults.option_b: Israel as a "kingdom of priests" (Exodus 19:6) receives God's blessing in order to mediate it to all nations
adults.option_c: The Aaronic blessing was only for the tribe of Levi
adults.option_d: The psalm rejects the Aaronic blessing as insufficient
adults.explanation: By echoing Numbers 6:24-26, Psalm 67 frames Israel's blessing within its priestly calling (Exodus 19:6) — blessed to be a blessing to all nations (cf. Genesis 12:3).

adults.question: What does "thy saving health" (Psalm 67:2) — literally "thy salvation" (yeshuah) — anticipate in redemptive history?
adults.option_a: The military victories of Israel's kings
adults.option_b: The universal proclamation of salvation through Yeshua (Jesus) to all nations
adults.option_c: The physical healing of all nations
adults.option_d: The agricultural prosperity described in verse 6
adults.explanation: The Hebrew yeshuah (saving health/salvation, Psalm 67:2) shares a root with Yeshua (Jesus) — pointing forward to the universal salvation Christ brings to all nations.

adults.question: What is the structural role of the refrain in Psalm 67:3 and 67:5?
adults.option_a: It marks liturgical pauses for the congregation to kneel
adults.option_b: It creates a concentric structure framing the reasons for universal praise — God's just rule (67:4) and his provision (67:6)
adults.option_c: It was sung by a different choir
adults.option_d: It divides the psalm into unrelated sections
adults.explanation: The refrain in Psalm 67:3 and 67:5 frames the central verse (67:4) about God's just governance of nations, creating a chiastic structure for the psalm.

adults.question: What two aspects of God's governance are highlighted in Psalm 67:4 as reasons for the nations to rejoice?
adults.option_a: His power and his wealth
adults.option_b: Righteous judgment of peoples and just governing of nations
adults.option_c: His mercy and his patience
adults.option_d: His creation and his providence
adults.explanation: Psalm 67:4 says the nations should rejoice because God "shalt judge the people righteously, and govern the nations upon earth" — just rule is cause for universal joy.

adults.question: How does the agricultural blessing of Psalm 67:6 function within the psalm's theological argument?
adults.option_a: It is merely a prayer for good harvests
adults.option_b: It connects God's material provision of Israel to the missionary goal — visible blessing testifies to God's character before the nations
adults.option_c: It replaces the need for the temple
adults.option_d: It is a covenant condition for Israel's obedience
adults.explanation: The earth's yield (Psalm 67:6) is not isolated — Israel's visible blessing by God testifies to his character and draws nations to him (cf. Deuteronomy 28:1-10).

adults.question: What does "God, even our own God" (Psalm 67:6) emphasize in the context of a psalm addressed to all nations?
adults.option_a: God belongs exclusively to Israel and not the nations
adults.option_b: The universal God who blesses all nations is also the personal covenant God of Israel — particularity within universality
adults.option_c: God has different identities for different peoples
adults.option_d: The nations have their own gods and Israel has hers
adults.explanation: The juxtaposition of "all nations" (Psalm 67:2) with "our own God" (Psalm 67:6) holds together God's universal sovereignty and his particular covenant relationship with Israel.

adults.question: How does Psalm 67 anticipate the Great Commission (Matthew 28:19)?
adults.option_a: It commands Israel to go to all nations with the Torah
adults.option_b: It prays for all nations to know God's salvation — the same missional goal as Christ's command to make disciples of all nations
adults.option_c: It predicts that Israel will rule all nations
adults.option_d: It has no connection to the New Testament
adults.explanation: Psalm 67's prayer for all nations to know God's saving way (Psalm 67:2) is the OT anticipation of the Great Commission — the same universal missional heart.

adults.question: What is the logical flow of Psalm 67's theology: blessing → knowledge → praise → more blessing?
adults.option_a: God blesses Israel, all nations see it, all nations praise God, and God blesses all the earth
adults.option_b: God punishes nations, they repent, they praise, and God blesses them
adults.option_c: Israel praises God, God blesses them, the nations learn from Israel, and God judges the nations
adults.option_d: Nations praise God first, then he blesses them, then Israel sees his power
adults.explanation: Psalm 67's logic is: God blesses Israel (67:1) → his way known among nations (67:2) → nations praise (67:3-5) → earth yields increase and all are blessed (67:6-7).

kids.question: What does Psalm 68:1 say will happen to God's enemies when God arises?
kids.option_d: They will flee to other lands
kids.explanation: David wrote, "Let God arise, let his enemies be scattered: let them also that hate him flee before him" (Psalm 68:1).

kids.question: What does Psalm 68:5 call God in relation to those who have no father?
kids.explanation: David declared that God is "A father of the fatherless, and a judge of the widows, is God in his holy habitation" (Psalm 68:5).

kids.question: What did Psalm 68:18 say God did on high — something Paul quotes in Ephesians 4:8?
kids.option_a: He built a throne in heaven
kids.option_b: He ascended on high and led captivity captive
kids.explanation: David wrote, "Thou hast ascended on high, thou hast led captivity captive: thou hast received gifts for men" (Psalm 68:18).

teens.question: What does Psalm 68 say God does for the solitary and the prisoners?
teens.option_a: He gives them strength to endure
teens.option_b: He sets the solitary in families and brings out prisoners into prosperity
teens.option_c: He sends angels to comfort them
teens.explanation: David wrote, "God setteth the solitary in families: he bringeth out those which are bound with chains" (Psalm 68:6).

teens.question: What natural phenomena accompanied God's march through the wilderness in Psalm 68?
teens.option_a: Fire and wind
teens.option_b: The earth shook and the heavens dropped rain
teens.explanation: David recalled, "The earth shook, the heavens also dropped at the presence of God: even Sinai itself was moved at the presence of God" (Psalm 68:8).

teens.question: How does Psalm 68:11 describe those who spread the good news of God's victory?
teens.option_a: As mighty warriors
teens.option_b: As a great company of those that published it
teens.option_d: As prophets sent to the nations
teens.explanation: David wrote, "The Lord gave the word: great was the company of those that published it" (Psalm 68:11).

teens.question: What mountain does Psalm 68:16 identify as the mountain God desires to dwell in?
teens.option_a: Mount Sinai
teens.explanation: David wrote, "This is the hill which God desireth to dwell in; yea, the LORD will dwell in it for ever" (Psalm 68:16) — referring to Zion.

teens.question: What does Psalm 68:20 say about God and the issues of death?
teens.option_a: Death is a punishment for the wicked only
teens.option_b: Unto God the Lord belong the issues from death
teens.option_c: God removes death from those who trust him
teens.option_d: Death comes only to those who rebel against God
teens.explanation: David declared, "Unto God the Lord belong the issues from death" (Psalm 68:20) — God controls the gates of death.

adults.question: How does Psalm 68:1 echo Numbers 10:35, and what is the theological significance of this echo?
adults.option_a: Both describe the ark of the covenant in battle
adults.option_b: Both use the priestly battle cry when the ark advanced, connecting Israel's worship march to God's warrior presence
adults.option_c: Both are thanksgiving psalms for military victory
adults.option_d: Both describe the return of the ark to Jerusalem
adults.explanation: Psalm 68:1 directly quotes Numbers 10:35 — "Rise up, LORD, and let thine enemies be scattered" — the ancient ark-march formula, situating Israel's worship in God's warrior-presence.

adults.question: Paul quotes Psalm 68:18 in Ephesians 4:8, applying it to Christ's ascension. What does "led captivity captive" mean in both the OT and NT contexts?
adults.option_a: Both refer to taking prisoners of war as slaves
adults.option_b: In the OT it describes God's triumph over enemies; in the NT Paul applies it to Christ's victory over sin, death, and spiritual powers at his ascension
adults.option_c: The verse only applies to Pentecost
adults.option_d: Paul misquotes the verse by changing "received" to "gave"
adults.explanation: Psalm 68:18's triumphal ascent (God leading captives) is applied by Paul (Ephesians 4:8) to Christ's ascension, which conquered spiritual enemies and distributed gifts to the church.

adults.question: What does the description of God as "father of the fatherless, and a judge of the widows" (Psalm 68:5) reveal about Israel's covenant ethics?
adults.option_a: God only cares for Israel's military strength
adults.option_b: God's character demands care for the most vulnerable — the fatherless and widow are special objects of his concern
adults.option_c: Widows and orphans were excluded from worship
adults.option_d: This is merely a poetic exaggeration
adults.explanation: God as father of the fatherless and defender of widows (Psalm 68:5) grounds Israel's social ethics in divine character — care for the vulnerable is a covenant obligation (cf. Deuteronomy 10:18).

adults.question: What is the significance of Mount Bashan's envy of Mount Zion in Psalm 68:15-16?
adults.option_a: Bashan was physically taller than Zion
adults.option_b: High, imposing mountains are superseded by small Zion — God's choice of the humble place over the impressive demonstrates his sovereign freedom
adults.option_c: Bashan was Israel's enemy territory
adults.option_d: The psalm describes a literal geological event
adults.explanation: The contrast between high Bashan and small Zion (Psalm 68:15-16) shows that God chooses the lowly — his dwelling is not determined by natural grandeur but by his sovereign will.

adults.question: What is the eschatological scope of Psalm 68:29-32, which anticipates kings bringing gifts to God?
adults.option_a: It is limited to Solomon's temple dedications
adults.option_b: It anticipates the eschatological ingathering of nations to Zion — a Gentile submission to God's kingship
adults.option_c: It describes the tribute of conquered enemies
adults.option_d: It refers to the return from Babylonian exile
adults.explanation: Psalm 68:29-32 anticipates kings from Egypt and Ethiopia bringing gifts to God (cf. Isaiah 60) — an eschatological vision of universal Gentile submission to Israel's God.

adults.question: How does Psalm 68's procession theology relate to the physical movement of the ark of the covenant?
adults.option_a: It is purely metaphorical and unrelated to the ark
adults.option_b: The psalm likely accompanied the ark's triumphal procession to Jerusalem, making God's physical march with his people the occasion for cosmic theology
adults.option_c: The ark is never mentioned in the psalm
adults.option_d: The psalm was written centuries after the ark was lost
adults.explanation: Psalm 68 is widely understood as a liturgical processional accompanying the ark — God's physical march with Israel (68:7-8) becomes the lens for cosmic theology.

adults.question: What does the list of tribes in Psalm 68:27 (Benjamin, Judah, Zebulun, Naphtali) represent?
adults.option_a: The four tribes that led Israel's armies
adults.option_b: A representative gathering of all Israel — north and south, small and great — united in worship
adults.option_c: The tribes that accompanied the ark from the wilderness
adults.option_d: The four cardinal points of Israel's tribal arrangement
adults.explanation: The four tribes (Psalm 68:27) represent all Israel — Benjamin and Judah from the south, Zebulun and Naphtali from the north — the whole nation united before God.

adults.question: What is the theological climax of Psalm 68, and where does it occur?
adults.option_a: The call to praise in verse 1
adults.option_b: The universal praise of all kingdoms and nations in verses 32-35, culminating in the declaration of God's strength and power
adults.option_c: The ascension of God in verse 18
adults.option_d: The defeat of enemies in verse 21
adults.explanation: Psalm 68 climaxes in a universal call for all kingdoms to sing to God (Psalm 68:32-35) — all the earth declaring his strength and power above all.

kids.question: What did the enemies give David to drink in Psalm 69?
kids.explanation: David wrote, "They gave me also gall for my meat; and in my thirst they gave me vinegar to drink" (Psalm 69:21).

kids.question: What did David say consumed him regarding God's house?
kids.option_a: A great love for its beauty
kids.option_c: A desire to serve as a priest
kids.option_d: A longing to see God's glory
kids.explanation: David said, "The zeal of thine house hath eaten me up" (Psalm 69:9).

kids.question: To what was David sinking in the opening of Psalm 69?
kids.explanation: David cried, "I sink in deep mire, where there is no standing: I am come into deep waters, where the floods overflow me" (Psalm 69:2).

teens.question: What does Psalm 69:9 say about the reproaches of those who reproached God?
teens.option_a: They were justly punished
teens.explanation: David wrote, "The reproaches of them that reproached thee are fallen upon me" (Psalm 69:9).

teens.question: What was David's condition that made him a stranger to his family in Psalm 69:8?
teens.option_a: His poverty
teens.option_b: His zeal for God made him an alien to his brethren and a stranger to his mother's children
teens.option_c: His long absence on a journey
teens.explanation: David wrote, "I am become a stranger unto my brethren, and an alien unto my mother's children" because of his zeal for God (Psalm 69:8-9).

teens.question: What did David say his enemies hated him without a cause for in Psalm 69:4?
teens.option_a: For his wealth
teens.option_b: They that hate me without a cause are more than the hairs of mine head
teens.explanation: David lamented, "They that hate me without a cause are more than the hairs of mine head" (Psalm 69:4).

teens.question: How does Psalm 69:21 connect to the crucifixion of Jesus?
teens.option_a: Jesus quoted this verse on the cross
teens.option_b: The vinegar given to Jesus at the cross (Matthew 27:48; John 19:29) fulfills this verse
teens.option_c: Jesus prayed this psalm in Gethsemane
teens.option_d: John cites it as fulfilled at the Last Supper
teens.explanation: Psalm 69:21 — "in my thirst they gave me vinegar to drink" — is fulfilled in John 19:29 when soldiers offered Jesus vinegar (sour wine) at the crucifixion.

teens.question: What does the psalmist say God will do for the humble and the poor in the closing section of Psalm 69:32-33?
teens.option_a: Give them wealth and honour
teens.option_b: The humble shall see and be glad; the LORD heareth the poor
teens.explanation: David declared, "The humble shall see this, and be glad... For the LORD heareth the poor, and despiseth not his prisoners" (Psalm 69:32-33).

adults.question: How many times is Psalm 69 quoted or alluded to in the New Testament, making it one of the most-cited OT texts?
adults.option_a: Twice
adults.option_b: About three times
adults.option_c: At least seven times
adults.option_d: Once
adults.explanation: Psalm 69 is one of the most-quoted psalms in the NT — cited at least seven times including Romans 11:9-10, John 2:17, John 15:25, Matthew 27:48, Acts 1:20, and Romans 15:3.

adults.question: John 2:17 quotes Psalm 69:9 in reference to Jesus cleansing the temple. What theological connection does John draw?
adults.option_a: Jesus was fulfilling a legal obligation
adults.option_b: Jesus' zeal for God's house consuming him identifies him as the messianic sufferer of Psalm 69 who bears the cost of God's honour
adults.option_c: Jesus was angry at the merchants personally
adults.option_d: John uses the verse to show Jesus loved the temple building
adults.explanation: John 2:17 applies Psalm 69:9 to Jesus — his consuming zeal for God's house identifies him as the messianic servant who bears divine reproach (John 2:17; cf. Romans 15:3).

adults.question: How does "they hated me without a cause" (Psalm 69:4) function in John 15:25?
adults.option_a: Jesus quotes it as a legal precedent
adults.option_b: Jesus applies it to himself — the world's hatred of him without cause fulfills Scripture, confirming his messianic identity
adults.option_c: John uses it as a general proverb
adults.option_d: It is used to defend the disciples' innocence
adults.explanation: Jesus directly applies Psalm 69:4 in John 15:25 — the world's baseless hatred of him "fulfilleth the word that is written in their law" — messianic fulfillment.

adults.question: What is the imprecatory nature of Psalm 69:22-28, and how has it been applied in the NT?
adults.option_a: It is a prayer for enemies' salvation
adults.option_b: It calls God's judgment on enemies; Acts 1:20 applies verse 25 to Judas Iscariot's fate
adults.option_c: It is purely historical and never applied in the NT
adults.option_d: It predicts the destruction of Jerusalem in 70 AD
adults.explanation: Psalm 69:25 ("Let their habitation be desolate") is quoted in Acts 1:20 as fulfilled in Judas Iscariot, whose betrayal brought upon him the judgment prophesied here.

adults.question: What does the opening water imagery (sinking in mire, floods, deep waters, Psalm 69:1-3, 14-15) represent as a metaphorical system?
adults.option_a: Literal drowning David experienced
adults.option_b: Chaotic, overwhelming suffering threatening to extinguish life — the deep waters symbolize mortal crisis beyond human rescue
adults.option_c: The waters of the Red Sea
adults.option_d: A spiritual purification ritual
adults.explanation: The deep waters and mire of Psalm 69:1-3, 14-15 are ancient Near Eastern chaos imagery — overwhelming, life-threatening suffering from which only God can save.

adults.question: What does "blot them out of the book of the living" (Psalm 69:28) imply about Israel's theology of divine records?
adults.option_a: It is merely a poetic wish
adults.option_b: It implies a heavenly register of the living from which the condemned are erased — anticipating the book of life in Revelation
adults.option_c: It refers to the genealogical records of Israel
adults.option_d: It is a priestly curse formula
adults.explanation: Psalm 69:28's book of the living anticipates the NT book of life (Revelation 3:5; 20:15) — a divine register of those in covenant relationship with God.

adults.question: How does Psalm 69:30-31 theologically reorder Israel's sacrificial system?
adults.option_a: It abolishes animal sacrifice entirely
adults.option_b: It subordinates animal sacrifice to the praise of a thankful heart — God is more pleased with grateful song than burnt offerings
adults.option_c: It argues that sacrifices are more important than praise
adults.option_d: It restricts praise to the Levitical singers
adults.explanation: Psalm 69:30-31 declares that praising God with a song pleases him more than an ox or bullock — an OT anticipation of the prophetic critique of empty ritual (cf. Psalm 51:16-17; Hosea 6:6).

adults.question: What does Psalm 69's structure (lament → imprecation → praise → eschatological hope) model for NT believers facing unjust suffering?
adults.option_a: That imprecatory prayer is forbidden in the NT
adults.option_b: That honest lament can coexist with praise, and God's ultimate justice transforms present suffering into eschatological hope
adults.option_c: That believers should suppress their complaints
adults.option_d: That suffering is always the result of personal sin
adults.explanation: Psalm 69 models the journey from raw lament (69:1-4) through honest prayer (69:5-28) to confident praise (69:30-36) — a model for NT believers facing unjust suffering (cf. Romans 8:18-25).

kids.question: What did David urgently ask God to do in the very first verse of Psalm 70?
kids.option_c: Send his angels to protect him
kids.explanation: David prayed, "Make haste, O God, to deliver me; make haste to help me, O LORD" (Psalm 70:1).

kids.question: What did David ask God to do to those who sought his life?
kids.option_b: Let them be ashamed and confounded
kids.option_c: Send them away to other lands
kids.option_d: Give them a chance to repent
kids.explanation: David prayed, "Let them be ashamed and confounded that seek after my soul" (Psalm 70:2).

kids.question: What did David call himself — showing his need for God?
kids.explanation: David concluded, "But I am poor and needy: make haste unto me, O God" (Psalm 70:5).

teens.question: What does David say the seekers of God should continually say?
teens.option_a: Bless the LORD all ye peoples
teens.option_c: Praise the LORD for he is good
teens.option_d: Holy is the LORD God Almighty
teens.explanation: David said, "Let all those that seek thee rejoice and be glad in thee: and let such as love thy salvation say continually, Let God be magnified" (Psalm 70:4).

teens.question: What does David pray about those who say "Aha, aha!" (mocking him)?
teens.option_a: That they will be converted
teens.option_b: That they will be turned backward and put to shame
teens.option_d: That they will be defeated in battle
teens.explanation: David prayed, "Let them be turned backward, and put to confusion, that desire my hurt... that say, Aha, aha" (Psalm 70:2-3).

teens.question: Psalm 70 is almost identical to which earlier psalm?
teens.explanation: Psalm 70 is nearly identical to Psalm 40:13-17 — both are urgent prayers for deliverance, with Psalm 70 being an independent liturgical use of that section.

teens.question: What does the brevity of Psalm 70 (only 5 verses) communicate about the nature of urgent prayer?
teens.option_a: Short prayers are less effective
teens.option_b: Genuine crisis prayer can be intense and brief — urgency strips prayer to its essentials
teens.option_c: David was too tired to write more
teens.explanation: Psalm 70's five-verse brevity captures the essence of crisis prayer — urgency and desperate need strip the prayer down to its bare essentials before God.

teens.question: What superscription or title does Psalm 70 carry that indicates its liturgical function?
teens.option_a: A prayer of the afflicted
teens.option_b: A psalm of David to bring to remembrance
teens.explanation: Psalm 70 is headed "A Psalm of David, to bring to remembrance" — suggesting it was used as a memorial/petition in Israel's liturgy.

adults.question: What does "to bring to remembrance" in Psalm 70's superscription suggest about its liturgical use?
adults.option_a: David wanted future generations to memorize it
adults.option_b: It was used in the context of the "memorial offering" (azkarah) — a ritual bringing the worshipper's need before God's attention
adults.option_c: It was a royal decree
adults.option_d: It was placed in the temple archives
adults.explanation: The title "to bring to remembrance" (Psalm 70 superscription) likely connects to the azkarah (memorial portion of the grain offering) — a liturgical bringing of oneself before God.

adults.question: How does Psalm 70's repetition of "make haste" (verses 1 and 5) function rhetorically?
adults.option_a: It shows David's impatience with God
adults.option_b: The double urgency frames the entire psalm — every word is under the pressure of desperate need
adults.option_c: It is a liturgical chorus
adults.option_d: It balances the two stanzas
adults.explanation: The twin urgency of "make haste" in Psalm 70:1 and 70:5 creates an envelope of crisis — the whole psalm breathes urgency and radical dependence on God.

adults.question: What does the contrast between the fate of enemies (ashamed, confounded, 70:2-3) and the righteous (glad, rejoicing, 70:4) reveal about OT theodicy?
adults.option_a: Righteous and wicked have identical destinies
adults.option_b: God's justice guarantees opposite outcomes — the wicked's shame is the necessary counterpart to the righteous's joy
adults.option_c: Righteousness guarantees immediate reward
adults.option_d: The wicked are not judged until death
adults.explanation: Psalm 70's sharp contrast — enemies confounded (70:2-3) vs. seekers rejoicing (70:4) — reflects OT theodicy: God's justice ensures that righteousness and wickedness lead to opposite ends.

adults.question: What is the theological significance of David's self-description as "poor and needy" (Psalm 70:5) for a king?
adults.option_a: David was literally impoverished at this time
adults.option_b: It expresses radical spiritual poverty — total dependence on God regardless of earthly status, the posture of the "anawim" (humble poor)
adults.option_c: It was a rhetorical device to gain sympathy
adults.option_d: It describes David's early pre-royal life
adults.explanation: David calling himself poor and needy (Psalm 70:5) despite being king reflects the anawim spirituality — the humble poor who depend entirely on God (cf. Matthew 5:3, "poor in spirit").

adults.question: When Psalm 70 is compared to Psalm 40:13-17, what does the independent use of this prayer section reveal?
adults.option_a: One version is a scribal error
adults.option_b: Individual psalm sections circulated independently and were reused in different liturgical contexts — scripture is organically living
adults.option_c: Psalm 70 corrects an error in Psalm 40
adults.option_d: David wrote Psalm 40 first and regretted it
adults.explanation: The existence of Psalm 70 as a standalone version of Psalm 40:13-17 shows that psalm sections were excerpted and reused liturgically — Israel's prayer treasury was fluid and living.

adults.question: How does "let God be magnified" (Psalm 70:4) function as a statement of priority within urgent crisis prayer?
adults.option_a: It delays God's help by praising first
adults.option_b: Even in desperate crisis, David's ultimate concern is God's magnification — the worshipper's need is subordinated to God's glory
adults.option_c: It is a standard liturgical formula
adults.option_d: It means David wants God to appear visibly
adults.explanation: David's cry "let God be magnified" (Psalm 70:4) within an urgent lament reveals that even crisis prayer is ultimately theocentric — God's glory is the supreme concern.

adults.question: What does the use of "LORD" (YHWH) in Psalm 70:1 and "God" (Elohim) in Psalm 70:1 and 5 reveal about the psalm's divine address?
adults.option_a: David is uncertain which name to use
adults.option_b: Both the personal covenant name (YHWH) and the generic divine name (Elohim) are used, appealing to God in both his covenant relationship and universal power
adults.option_c: The names are interchangeable with no significance
adults.option_d: YHWH is used for praise and Elohim for petition
adults.explanation: Psalm 70 addresses both YHWH (covenant name, 70:1) and Elohim (universal name, 70:1, 5), appealing to both God's personal covenant faithfulness and his omnipotent power.

adults.question: How does Psalm 70's raw urgency contribute to the Psalter's honest theology of prayer?
adults.option_a: It models polished, formal prayer
adults.option_b: It validates desperate, unadorned cries to God — the Psalter permits and models raw urgency as legitimate prayer
adults.option_c: It shows that only emotional prayers are answered
adults.option_d: It warns against praying too urgently
adults.explanation: Psalm 70's unadorned urgency contributes to the Psalter's honest prayer theology — it legitimises stripped-down crisis prayer as fully valid before God (cf. Romans 8:26).

kids.question: What did the psalmist ask God not to do in his old age in Psalm 71?
kids.option_b: Cast him off in the time of old age
kids.explanation: The psalmist prayed, "Cast me not off in the time of old age; forsake me not when my strength faileth" (Psalm 71:9).

kids.question: What did the psalmist say he had leaned on since his birth?
kids.option_b: God who took him out of his mother's womb
kids.option_c: The strength of his own body
kids.explanation: The psalmist said, "By thee have I been holden up from the womb: thou art he that took me out of my mother's bowels" (Psalm 71:6).

kids.question: What did the psalmist say he would always hope in and praise God more and more?
kids.explanation: The psalmist declared, "But I will hope continually, and will yet praise thee more and more" (Psalm 71:14).

teens.question: What did the psalmist's enemies say when they plotted against him in Psalm 71:11?
teens.option_a: God has given him to us
teens.option_b: God hath forsaken him: persecute and take him; for there is none to deliver him
teens.option_c: He has no friends to help him
teens.option_d: His God will not hear his prayer
teens.explanation: The psalmist's enemies said, "God hath forsaken him: persecute and take him; for there is none to deliver him" (Psalm 71:11).

teens.question: What does the psalmist say he will shew to this generation and everyone to come?
teens.option_a: The law of Moses
teens.explanation: The psalmist declared, "I will shew thy strength unto this generation, and thy power to every one that is to come" (Psalm 71:18).

teens.question: What musical instrument does the psalmist mention praising God with in Psalm 71?
teens.option_a: The trumpet
teens.explanation: The psalmist declared, "I will also praise thee with the psaltery, even thy truth, O my God: unto thee will I sing with the harp" (Psalm 71:22).

teens.question: What does the psalmist ask God to do after showing him troubles and adversities in Psalm 71?
teens.option_a: Give him peace at last
teens.option_b: Quicken him again and bring him up again from the depths of the earth
teens.explanation: The psalmist prayed, "Thou, which hast shewed me great and sore troubles, shalt quicken me again, and shalt bring me up again from the depths of the earth" (Psalm 71:20).

teens.question: How does the psalmist describe God as his refuge from his youth in Psalm 71:5?
teens.option_a: As his daily bread and water
teens.option_b: As his hope and his trust from his youth
teens.option_c: As the guide of all his ways
teens.option_d: As the teacher of his people
teens.explanation: The psalmist said, "For thou art my hope, O Lord GOD: thou art my trust from my youth" (Psalm 71:5).

adults.question: What is the likely setting of Psalm 71 and what makes it distinctive among the Psalms?
adults.option_a: A young warrior going into battle
adults.option_b: It is the prayer of an elderly believer — the only psalm explicitly voiced from the perspective of old age and lifelong faith
adults.option_c: A king celebrating his coronation
adults.option_d: A priest performing his annual duties
adults.explanation: Psalm 71 is unique as the prayer of an aged person (Psalm 71:9, 18) — a lifelong believer reviewing God's faithfulness from birth to old age.

adults.question: What does the psalmist's phrase "thou art my strong refuge" (Psalm 71:7) — using the Hebrew "mahseh" — convey about his theology?
adults.option_a: God is physically protective like a wall
adults.option_b: Mahseh (refuge/shelter) describes God as a place of total security — the aged believer's whole life has been spent under divine shelter
adults.option_c: God rescued the psalmist from a specific battle
adults.option_d: God is the refuge only in times of crisis
adults.explanation: Mahseh (Psalm 71:7) is a rich refuge word — not just emergency protection but a lifelong dwelling in God's sheltering care from womb to old age.

adults.question: How does Psalm 71's interlacing of petition and praise (e.g., 71:1-4 petition, 71:5-8 praise, 71:9-13 petition) model the rhythm of mature prayer?
adults.option_a: It shows that the psalmist forgot his train of thought
adults.option_b: It models prayer as a living dialogue where trust and need alternate — praise does not wait until deliverance but interrupts lament
adults.option_c: The sections were originally separate prayers
adults.option_d: Petition and praise are kept strictly separate
adults.explanation: Psalm 71's interweaving of petition and praise models mature prayer — praise interrupts lament (71:5-8) because faith does not wait for deliverance to worship God.

adults.question: What does "I will go in the strength of the Lord GOD" (Psalm 71:16) declare about the aged believer's source of ongoing vitality?
adults.option_a: Physical strength is restored to the aged
adults.option_b: In the absence of youthful strength, God's strength becomes the sole source of the aged believer's forward movement
adults.option_c: The psalmist is beginning a military campaign
adults.option_d: God gives the aged special supernatural ability
adults.explanation: Psalm 71:16's "I will go in the strength of the Lord GOD" is a declaration of radical dependence — the aged believer's vitality comes entirely from God, not from self.

adults.question: What does the psalmist's request in Psalm 71:18 — "forsake me not...until I have shewed thy strength unto this generation" — reveal about his understanding of his life's purpose in old age?
adults.option_a: Old age has no remaining purpose
adults.option_b: The aged believer's final purpose is testimony — to pass the knowledge of God's power to the next generation
adults.option_c: The psalmist fears death before completing temple service
adults.option_d: God abandons the elderly when their strength fails
adults.explanation: Psalm 71:18's request to live until he can testify to the next generation reveals that the aged believer's final vocation is intergenerational witness — faith passed to the next generation.

adults.question: How does the psalmist's reference to being held from the womb (71:6) relate to his trust in God in old age?
adults.option_a: It is a nostalgic memory
adults.option_b: It creates a theological arc: if God was faithful from birth, he will be faithful in old age — the whole life as evidence for continued trust
adults.option_c: Birth and old age are unrelated in the psalm
adults.option_d: It refers to a miraculous birth like Isaac or Samuel
adults.explanation: Psalm 71's womb-to-old-age arc (71:6, 9, 18) builds a cumulative argument: God's faithfulness across an entire life is the basis for trust in life's final chapter.

adults.question: What is the significance of the psalmist's vow to praise God with "lips that rejoice" (Psalm 71:23) when God restores him?
adults.option_a: Praise is contingent on restoration
adults.option_b: The lips that cried in lament will become the lips of praise — suffering and praise are part of one continuous testimony
adults.option_c: Praise is only appropriate after complete victory
adults.option_d: The psalmist had previously been silenced
adults.explanation: Psalm 71:23's rejoicing lips complete the journey from the crying soul of 71:2-3 — the same mouth that begged becomes the mouth that praises, unifying lament and doxology.

adults.question: How does Psalm 71 relate to the broader wisdom tradition's teaching on the value of old age?
adults.option_a: Old age is seen as a burden without spiritual value
adults.option_b: It affirms the wisdom tradition's view (Proverbs 16:31; 20:29) that old age can be a crown of lifelong faithfulness — a platform for testimony
adults.option_c: The psalm is pessimistic about aging
adults.option_d: Psalm 71 contradicts Proverbs on old age
adults.explanation: Psalm 71 aligns with wisdom tradition (Proverbs 16:31 — "hoary head is a crown of glory") by presenting old age as the culmination of faithful life and a platform for testimony.

kids.question: How far will the king's dominion reach according to Psalm 72?
kids.option_b: From sea to sea and to the ends of the earth
kids.option_d: From the north to the south only
kids.explanation: The psalmist prayed, "He shall have dominion also from sea to sea, and from the river unto the ends of the earth" (Psalm 72:8).

kids.question: What does the psalmist say the king will do for the poor and needy?
kids.option_b: Save the children of the needy and break in pieces the oppressor
kids.explanation: The psalmist prayed, "He shall save the children of the needy, and shall break in pieces the oppressor" (Psalm 72:4).

kids.question: What is said about the king's name in Psalm 72?
kids.option_a: It shall be feared by all nations
kids.option_b: His name shall endure for ever
kids.option_c: It shall be written in the heavens
kids.option_d: All kings shall know his name
kids.explanation: The psalmist declared, "His name shall endure for ever: his name shall be continued as long as the sun" (Psalm 72:17).

teens.question: What two things does the king judge righteously in Psalm 72?
teens.option_a: The rich and the powerful
teens.option_b: The poor and the children of the needy
teens.explanation: The psalmist prayed that the king would "judge thy people with righteousness, and thy poor with judgment" (Psalm 72:2) and deliver the needy.

teens.question: What natural image illustrates the king's refreshing rule over the people in Psalm 72:6?
teens.option_a: A mighty wind that cools the land
teens.option_b: He shall come down like rain upon the mown grass
teens.option_c: A river of water in a dry land
teens.option_d: A spring that never runs dry
teens.explanation: The psalmist said, "He shall come down like rain upon the mown grass: as showers that water the earth" (Psalm 72:6).

teens.question: Who does Psalm 72 say shall bow before the king — specifically mentioning nations from the south?
teens.option_a: The kings of the north only
teens.option_b: The kings of Sheba and Seba shall offer gifts, all kings shall fall down before him
teens.option_d: The people of Egypt and Babylon
teens.explanation: The psalmist prayed, "The kings of Sheba and Seba shall offer gifts... all kings shall fall down before him: all nations shall serve him" (Psalm 72:10-11).

teens.question: What phrase closes Book II of the Psalms (Psalms 42-72) at the end of Psalm 72?
teens.option_a: The LORD is my shepherd
teens.option_b: The prayers of David the son of Jesse are ended
teens.option_c: Let all the earth be filled with his glory
teens.option_d: Praise ye the LORD for ever and ever
teens.explanation: Psalm 72:20 reads, "The prayers of David the son of Jesse are ended" — the editorial colophon closing Book II of the Psalter.

teens.question: What does the psalmist say the king will do to the man in need who has no helper in Psalm 72:12?
teens.option_a: Give him a judge to plead his case
teens.option_b: Deliver him; he shall spare the poor and needy
teens.option_c: Send him to the priests for help
teens.option_d: Give him a field to work
teens.explanation: The psalmist prayed, "He shall deliver the needy when he crieth; the poor also, and him that hath no helper" (Psalm 72:12).

adults.question: What is the dual horizon of Psalm 72 — which two kings does it simultaneously address?
adults.option_a: David and his son Absalom
adults.option_b: Solomon historically and the Messiah eschatologically — it is both a royal coronation prayer and a Messianic prophecy
adults.option_c: David and Moses
adults.option_d: Solomon and Hezekiah
adults.explanation: Psalm 72 is simultaneously a prayer for Solomon at his coronation (its historical horizon) and a Messianic prophecy (its eschatological horizon) — no earthly king fully fulfills its vision.

adults.question: How does "he shall have dominion from sea to sea" (Psalm 72:8) connect to the Abrahamic covenant and its land promises?
adults.option_a: It refers to Israel's conquest of Canaan
adults.option_b: It expands the land promise beyond Canaan to universal sovereignty — the king is the heir of all God's covenant promises to Abraham
adults.option_c: It describes the borders of Solomon's kingdom
adults.option_d: It is merely poetic hyperbole
adults.explanation: Psalm 72:8's universal dominion echoes the Abrahamic promise (Genesis 22:17-18) that Abraham's seed would possess the gates of enemies — the king is the covenant heir.

adults.question: What does the abundance-of-grain image (Psalm 72:16 — "handful of corn on top of mountains") convey about the ideal king's reign?
adults.option_a: Israel will become an agricultural superpower
adults.option_b: Messianic rule brings abundant, impossible fruitfulness — even barren mountaintops overflow with grain, reversing the curse
adults.option_c: The king will be known for farming innovations
adults.option_d: The mountains will be terraced for agriculture
adults.explanation: Grain on mountain tops (Psalm 72:16) is a hyperbole of impossible abundance — Messianic rule reverses the curse and creates superabundant shalom (cf. Amos 9:13).

adults.question: How does Paul connect the universal blessing through the king's name (Psalm 72:17 — "all nations shall be blessed in him") to the Abrahamic covenant in Galatians 3?
adults.option_a: Paul does not connect these texts
adults.option_b: Psalm 72:17 echoes Genesis 12:3 ("all families of the earth shall be blessed") — the Messianic king is the Seed of Abraham through whom all nations are blessed
adults.option_c: The blessing is limited to nations that accept the Torah
adults.option_d: The blessing refers to material prosperity only
adults.explanation: Psalm 72:17's "all nations shall be blessed in him" directly echoes Genesis 12:3 — the Messianic king is identified as the Abrahamic Seed through whom universal blessing comes (cf. Galatians 3:16).

adults.question: What is the ethical centre of Psalm 72's vision of ideal kingship?
adults.option_a: Military conquest and territorial expansion
adults.option_b: Justice for the poor, needy, and oppressed — the king's greatness is measured by his care for the vulnerable
adults.option_c: Wisdom and learning above all virtues
adults.option_d: Wealth and international tribute
adults.explanation: The ethical centre of Psalm 72 is justice for the poor and oppressed (72:2, 4, 12-14) — the ideal king's greatness is measured not by conquest but by how he protects the vulnerable.

adults.question: What does "he shall live" (Psalm 72:15) in the context of the psalm's universalism suggest about the Messianic king's life?
adults.option_a: The king will simply have a long reign
adults.option_b: It anticipates the eternal life of the Messianic king whose reign has no end — fulfilled in Christ's resurrection
adults.option_c: It is a prayer for Solomon's health
adults.option_d: It means the king will survive his enemies
adults.explanation: Psalm 72:15's "he shall live" within the context of eternal dominion (72:5, 17) points beyond any mortal king to the Messiah whose life and reign are eternal.

adults.question: What is the doxological function of Psalm 72:18-19 within the Psalter?
adults.option_a: It concludes only Psalm 72
adults.option_b: It is the closing doxology of Book II (Psalms 42-72), a liturgical benediction praising God whose glory fills the whole earth
adults.option_c: It introduces Book III
adults.option_d: It is a separate psalm misplaced here
adults.explanation: Psalm 72:18-19 is the closing doxology of Book II of the Psalter (Psalms 42-72), blessing God and praying for his glory to fill all the earth.

adults.question: How does the image of the king defending the poor (Psalm 72:12-14) — "their blood shall be precious in his sight" — anticipate Christ's redemptive work?
adults.option_a: It is limited to economic welfare programs
adults.option_b: The king who redeems the weak from oppression and counts their blood precious foreshadows Christ who redeems at the cost of his own precious blood
adults.option_c: It describes Solomon's judicial reforms
adults.option_d: It is a military metaphor for protecting citizens
adults.explanation: The king's redemption of the needy with precious blood (Psalm 72:14) foreshadows Christ whose own blood (1 Peter 1:19) is the price of redemption for the weak and helpless.

kids.question: Who wrote Psalm 73?
kids.explanation: The heading says "A Psalm of Asaph." Asaph was a worship leader appointed by David.

kids.question: What almost happened to Asaph when he saw wicked people doing well?
kids.option_a: His feet almost slipped — he nearly lost his faith
kids.option_c: He ran away from the temple
kids.explanation: Psalm 73:2 — "But as for me, my feet were almost gone; my steps had well nigh slipped."

kids.question: Where did Asaph finally understand why the wicked prosper?
kids.explanation: Psalm 73:17 — "Until I went into the sanctuary of God; then understood I their end."

teens.question: What troubled Asaph about the wicked in Psalm 73:3-5?
teens.option_a: They prospered with no troubles — healthy, strong, and free from common burdens
teens.explanation: Psalm 73:3-5 — "I was envious at the foolish, when I saw the prosperity of the wicked...They are not in trouble as other men."

teens.question: What did the wicked arrogantly say about God in Psalm 73:11?
teens.option_a: "How doth God know? and is there knowledge in the most High?"
teens.option_b: "God is great and we fear Him"
teens.option_d: "God will reward us for our deeds"
teens.explanation: Psalm 73:11 — The wicked deny that God sees or knows what they do.

teens.question: What realization changed Asaph's perspective?
teens.option_a: He understood their final end — they are set in slippery places and cast down to destruction
teens.option_b: He received more wealth than them
teens.explanation: Psalm 73:17-19 — "Then understood I their end. Surely thou didst set them in slippery places."

teens.question: What beautiful declaration does Asaph make in Psalm 73:25?
teens.option_a: "Whom have I in heaven but thee? and there is none upon earth that I desire beside thee"
teens.option_b: "The Lord is my shepherd I shall not want"
teens.option_c: "Create in me a clean heart O God"
teens.option_d: "The heavens declare the glory of God"
teens.explanation: Psalm 73:25 — One of the most devotional verses in the entire Psalter.

teens.question: What does Asaph conclude is good for him at the end?
teens.option_a: Drawing near to God and putting his trust in the Lord
teens.option_c: Leaving Israel for a better land
teens.explanation: Psalm 73:28 — "But it is good for me to draw near to God: I have put my trust in the Lord GOD."

adults.question: How does Psalm 73 function as the theological introduction to Book III of the Psalter (Psalms 73-89)?
adults.option_a: It opens Book III with a crisis of theodicy — the prosperity of the wicked versus the suffering of the righteous — setting the theme for the darkest section of the Psalter, dominated by national lament
adults.option_b: It is simply the next psalm numerically
adults.option_c: It introduces temple worship songs
adults.option_d: It serves as a personal diary with no broader significance
adults.explanation: Book III (Ps 73-89) is the darkest section of the Psalter, reflecting exilic crisis. Psalm 73 opens with the foundational theodicy question that frames the entire book, culminating in Psalm 89's cry over God's apparent abandonment of the Davidic covenant.

adults.question: What is the significance of Asaph's confession "I was as a beast before thee" (Psalm 73:22)?
adults.option_a: It acknowledges that envying the wicked was sub-rational — reducing himself to animal-level thinking that evaluates life purely by visible material outcomes rather than by faith; the sanctuary visit restored spiritual cognition
adults.option_b: He literally believed he turned into an animal
adults.option_c: He is comparing himself to sacrificial animals
adults.option_d: It is a standard humility formula
adults.explanation: Psalm 73:22 — behemoth (beast/brute). Evaluating reality by sight alone (material prosperity) is beastly cognition. The sanctuary (v.17) restores the capacity to see beyond the visible to the eternal.

adults.question: How does Psalm 73:24 — "Thou shalt guide me with thy counsel, and afterward receive me to glory" — contribute to OT eschatology?
adults.option_a: The phrase "afterward receive me to glory" suggests post-death reception into God's glorious presence, providing one of the clearest OT intimations of afterlife hope, parallel to Psalm 49:15 and the Enoch tradition (Gen 5:24, same verb laqach)
adults.option_b: It refers to receiving earthly honours
adults.option_c: Glory means military victory
adults.option_d: The verse only describes guidance in this life
adults.explanation: Psalm 73:24 — "afterward receive me to glory." The verb laqach (take/receive) is the same used for Enoch (Gen 5:24). Combined with 73:26 — "my portion for ever" — the psalm envisions permanent relationship with God beyond death.

adults.question: What does the sanctuary as the epistemological turning point (Psalm 73:17) reveal about worship and understanding?
adults.option_a: Certain truths are inaccessible through isolated reason and become visible only in the context of worship — communal worship reorients cognition from horizontal comparison to vertical encounter
adults.option_b: The sanctuary gave Asaph a quiet place to think logically
adults.option_c: He received a prophetic vision there
adults.option_d: The sanctuary had scrolls with answers
adults.explanation: Psalm 73:16-17 — Reason alone (v.16) produced pain. Worship (v.17) produced understanding. Worship provides an epistemological context that pure reason cannot.

adults.question: How does Asaph's movement from envy to supreme devotion parallel the book of Job?
adults.option_a: Both follow the same trajectory: observing unjust prosperity → crisis of faith → encounter with God → transformed perspective that finds God Himself as the supreme good; both resolve theodicy relationally, not intellectually
adults.option_b: The two texts have nothing in common
adults.option_c: Asaph agrees with Job's friends
adults.option_d: They address completely different topics
adults.explanation: Psalm 73 parallels Job: crisis over the wicked's prosperity (73:3 ≈ Job 21:7-13), near despair (73:13 ≈ Job 9:22), turning point through encounter (73:17 ≈ Job 42:5), resolution in God Himself (73:25 ≈ Job 42:5-6). Both conclude that God's presence, not explanations, resolves faith crises.

adults.question: What is the theological import of "God is the strength of my heart, and my portion for ever" (Psalm 73:26)?
adults.option_a: By claiming God as his "portion" (cheleq), Asaph uses Levitical inheritance language — the Levites received no land because God was their portion (Num 18:20); this democratizes the Levitical privilege, making God-as-inheritance available to every believer
adults.option_b: Portion simply means God gives him food
adults.option_c: It refers to land inheritance
adults.option_d: It is a conventional formula
adults.explanation: Psalm 73:26 — chelqi (my portion). Num 18:20 — "I am thy part and thine inheritance." Asaph extends the Levitical principle: God-as-portion is the highest possible inheritance. Lam 3:24 — "The LORD is my portion, saith my soul."

adults.question: How does the wicked's end as "a dream when one awaketh" (Psalm 73:20) inform the psalm's reality theology?
adults.option_a: It inverts apparent reality: the wicked's prosperity that seemed solid is actually the dream, while the righteous sufferer's relationship with God is true reality — what appears real to observation dissolves, while what appears invisible persists eternally
adults.option_b: It means the wicked will have nightmares
adults.option_c: It predicts the wicked will die in sleep
adults.option_d: It refers to God waking from inattention
adults.explanation: Psalm 73:20 — Material prosperity = dream (temporary, illusory). God's presence = waking reality (permanent). 2 Cor 4:18 — "the things which are seen are temporal; but the things which are not seen are eternal."

adults.question: What does Asaph's concern that speaking his doubt would "offend against the generation of thy children" (Psalm 73:15) reveal about faith's communal dimension?
adults.option_a: Faith is not purely individual — theological doubt affects the community; Asaph restrains public doubt not from dishonesty but from pastoral responsibility, recognizing his crisis could undermine weaker believers' faith
adults.option_b: He feared punishment by authorities
adults.option_c: He was embarrassed, not concerned for others
adults.option_d: The generation of God's children refers to angels
adults.explanation: Psalm 73:15 — Asaph holds private doubt and public responsibility in tension. He processes doubt within the sanctuary (v.17) rather than broadcasting it. Cf. Rom 14:13 — "that no man put a stumblingblock...in his brother's way."

kids.question: What sad thing happened to God's sanctuary in Psalm 74?
kids.option_a: The enemy destroyed it and set it on fire
kids.option_b: It was filled with beautiful decorations
kids.option_c: It was moved to a new city
kids.option_d: Animals came to live inside it
kids.explanation: Psalm 74:3,7 — "The enemy hath done wickedly in the sanctuary...They have cast fire into thy sanctuary."

kids.question: What powerful things did Asaph say God did to the sea?
kids.option_a: He divided the sea and broke the dragons' heads in the waters
kids.option_b: He made the sea completely dry
kids.option_c: He turned the sea into fresh water
kids.option_d: He filled the sea with gold
kids.explanation: Psalm 74:13 — "Thou didst divide the sea by thy strength: thou brakest the heads of the dragons in the waters."

kids.question: What did Asaph ask God to remember?
kids.option_a: His congregation that He had purchased long ago
kids.option_b: The songs they sang in the temple
kids.option_c: The food they offered as sacrifices
kids.explanation: Psalm 74:2 — "Remember thy congregation, which thou hast purchased of old."

teens.question: What complaint does Asaph make about prophetic signs in Psalm 74:9?
teens.option_a: "We see not our signs; there is no more any prophet; neither is there among us any that knoweth how long"
teens.option_b: There are too many prophets giving contradictory messages
teens.option_c: The signs are coming too quickly
teens.option_d: People do not cry loud enough
teens.explanation: Psalm 74:9 — A cry of prophetic silence during national catastrophe.

teens.question: What does Asaph say God established in Psalm 74:16-17?
teens.option_a: Day and night, the sun, the borders of the earth, summer and winter
teens.explanation: Psalm 74:16-17 — "The day is thine, the night also is thine...Thou hast set all the borders of the earth: thou hast made summer and winter."

teens.question: What does Asaph ask God not to forget in Psalm 74:19?
teens.option_a: The life of His turtledove — not to deliver it to the wicked
teens.explanation: Psalm 74:19 — "O deliver not the soul of thy turtledove unto the multitude of the wicked."

teens.question: What appeal does Asaph make in Psalm 74:22?
teens.option_a: "Arise, O God, plead thine own cause — remember how the foolish man reproaches thee daily"
teens.option_b: Please send rain upon the crops
teens.explanation: Psalm 74:22 — "Arise, O God, plead thine own cause."

teens.question: What imagery does Psalm 74:5-6 use to describe the temple's destruction?
teens.option_a: Enemies lifting axes like chopping thick trees — hacking the carved woodwork
teens.option_b: A flood washing away the foundations
teens.explanation: Psalm 74:5-6 — "A man was famous according as he had lifted up axes upon the thick trees. But now they break down the carved work thereof."

adults.question: How does Psalm 74's reference to God breaking Leviathan's heads (v.14) connect to Job 41?
adults.option_a: Both use the Leviathan motif differently: Job 41 presents Leviathan as God's untameable creature; Psalm 74 recalls God's historical defeat of chaos at creation/exodus — together they show God as both Creator and Conqueror of chaotic forces
adults.option_b: The two texts contradict each other
adults.option_c: Psalm 74 refers to a literal sea creature
adults.option_d: Both describe the same event at the Red Sea
adults.explanation: Psalm 74:13-14 recasts creation/exodus as cosmic victory over chaos. Job 41 shows God governing Leviathan; Psalm 74 remembers God defeating it. Together: God creates, governs, and conquers chaos.

adults.question: What is the theological significance of "there is no more any prophet" (Psalm 74:9)?
adults.option_a: It describes prophetic silence where God's people have no mediating voice — intensifying the lament and anticipating periods of divine silence (e.g., the 400 years between testaments) where faith must persist without fresh revelation
adults.option_b: The prophets had all died of old age
adults.option_c: It contradicts other psalms
adults.option_d: People stopped listening
adults.explanation: Psalm 74:9 — Amos 8:11 — "a famine...of hearing the words of the LORD." Prophetic absence during catastrophe creates maximum theological distress: suffering without interpretation.

adults.question: How does the appeal to covenant (Psalm 74:20) function as the psalm's theological leverage?
adults.option_a: By invoking the covenant, Asaph shifts from emotional plea to legal argument — God has bound Himself by covenant, and the temple's destruction appears to violate it; the appeal is juridical, calling God to account for His own commitments
adults.option_b: The covenant reference is vague sentiment
adults.option_c: Asaph negotiates a new covenant
adults.option_d: It is the Noahic covenant about floods
adults.explanation: Psalm 74:20 — "Have respect unto the covenant." The covenant (berit) is the Abrahamic/Davidic covenant. By invoking it, Asaph provides theological grounds for intervention: God's own faithfulness demands action.

adults.question: What does the juxtaposition of cosmic creation (vv.13-17) with national destruction (vv.3-8) achieve?
adults.option_a: A deliberately jarring contrast: the God who split the sea and crushed Leviathan is the same God whose sanctuary lies in ruins — the gap between divine power and present reality IS the lament
adults.option_b: The creation section is a later insertion
adults.option_c: It proves God has grown weaker
adults.option_d: The juxtaposition is accidental
adults.explanation: The psalm alternates: destruction (vv.3-8) → silence (v.9) → creation power (vv.13-17) → appeal (vv.18-23). The creation recital is argument: You who did THIS cannot be indifferent to THAT.

adults.question: How does the turtledove metaphor (Psalm 74:19) illuminate Israel's self-understanding during crisis?
adults.option_a: The turtledove is vulnerable, gentle, and covenantally significant (used in sacrifice, Gen 15:9) — Israel sees itself as a fragile bird handed over to predators, with vulnerability language that is both honest and theologically loaded with sacrificial overtones
adults.option_b: It was Asaph's favourite bird
adults.option_c: Doves are common in the region
adults.option_d: No theological significance
adults.explanation: Psalm 74:19 — The tor (turtledove) appears in Abrahamic covenant sacrifice (Gen 15:9), purification offerings (Lev 12:6), and as a love symbol (Song 2:12). Israel as God's turtledove is simultaneously sacrificial victim, covenant partner, and beloved.

adults.question: What is the canonical significance of Psalm 74 after the triumph of Psalm 73?
adults.option_a: Psalm 73's individual resolution is immediately challenged by collective catastrophe — personal faith must coexist with communal devastation; Book III progressively deepens the tension between individual trust and national crisis toward the Davidic covenant crisis of Psalm 89
adults.option_b: Placement is purely numerical
adults.option_c: Psalm 74 undoes Psalm 73
adults.option_d: Book III has no organizing principle
adults.explanation: The sequence: Ps 73 (individual theodicy resolved) → Ps 74 (national faith in crisis). Solving theodicy personally does not eliminate communal suffering. Book III's arc traces deepening corporate crisis.

adults.question: How does Psalm 74:12 function as the psalm's theological fulcrum?
adults.option_a: It pivots from lament to confidence: "my King" personalizes the sovereign; "of old" appeals to track record; "working salvation in the midst of the earth" insists God acts within history — grounding the creation recital in historical, not merely mythological, categories
adults.option_b: It is a transition verse with minimal weight
adults.option_c: It contradicts the lament
adults.option_d: It refers to an earthly king
adults.explanation: Psalm 74:12 — (1) "my King" — personal relationship. (2) "of old" — appeal to precedent. (3) "in the midst of the earth" — God works within creation. This transforms the creation recital from cosmogony to soteriology.

adults.question: What does "plead thine own cause" (Psalm 74:22) reveal about the nature of Israel's destruction as a theological problem?
adults.option_a: It reframes Israel's suffering as God's problem: if Israel is destroyed, God's reputation is mocked; the destruction is not merely humanitarian crisis but crisis of divine honour — God must act for His own name's sake
adults.option_b: Asaph selfishly asks God to avenge personal enemies
adults.option_c: The plea has no theological depth
adults.option_d: Asaph suggests God needs a lawyer
adults.explanation: Psalm 74:22-23 — Ezek 36:22 — "I do not this for your sakes...but for mine holy name's sake." Asaph deploys the divine-honour argument: Israel's fate and God's reputation are inseparable.

kids.question: Who holds up the pillars of the earth according to Psalm 75?
kids.explanation: Psalm 75:3 — "I bear up the pillars of it." God sustains the earth.

kids.question: What does God tell the wicked not to lift up?
kids.option_a: Their horn — meaning their proud power
kids.explanation: Psalm 75:4-5 — "Lift not up the horn." The horn symbolizes proud strength.

kids.question: What is in God's hand according to Psalm 75?
kids.option_a: A cup of wine that the wicked must drink
kids.explanation: Psalm 75:8 — "For in the hand of the LORD there is a cup, and the wine is red."

teens.question: Where does promotion and authority come from according to Psalm 75:6-7?
teens.option_a: Not from east, west, or south — God is the judge who puts down one and sets up another
teens.explanation: Psalm 75:6-7 — "Promotion cometh neither from the east, nor from the west, nor from the south. But God is the judge."

teens.question: What does the cup in God's hand represent?
teens.option_a: Divine judgment — the wicked must drink it to the dregs
teens.option_b: A cup of blessing for the righteous
teens.option_c: The cup used in temple worship
teens.option_d: A cup of water for the thirsty
teens.explanation: Psalm 75:8 — "the dregs thereof, all the wicked of the earth shall wring them out, and drink them." Cf. Jer 25:15 and Rev 14:10.

teens.question: When does God say He will judge in Psalm 75:2?
teens.option_a: When He receives the congregation — at His appointed time He will judge uprightly
teens.option_b: At the end of every year
teens.option_c: Only on the Day of Atonement
teens.explanation: Psalm 75:2 — "When I shall receive the congregation I will judge uprightly."

teens.question: What happens to the horns of the wicked according to Psalm 75:10?
teens.option_a: All the horns of the wicked will be cut off, but the righteous horn shall be exalted
teens.option_b: The wicked grow even more powerful
teens.option_d: They are given to the righteous as trophies
teens.explanation: Psalm 75:10 — "All the horns of the wicked also will I cut off; but the horns of the righteous shall be exalted."

teens.question: What does Asaph promise to do in response to God's justice?
teens.option_a: Declare forever and sing praises to the God of Jacob
teens.option_c: Gather an army against the wicked
teens.explanation: Psalm 75:9 — "But I will declare for ever; I will sing praises to the God of Jacob."

adults.question: How does the cup metaphor in Psalm 75:8 connect to Christ's Gethsemane prayer?
adults.option_a: The judgment cup that the wicked must drink is the same cup Christ asked to pass from Him (Matt 26:39) — He drank the judgment cup on behalf of sinners, transforming the image from judgment on the wicked to substitutionary atonement
adults.option_b: The cups are completely unrelated
adults.option_c: Christ's cup was literal wine
adults.option_d: The Gethsemane cup is only physical suffering
adults.explanation: Psalm 75:8 — judgment cup. Isa 51:17 — "cup of his fury." Matt 26:39 — "let this cup pass from me." Christ willingly drank the judgment cup meant for sinners.

adults.question: What does the absence of "north" in the list of directions (v.6) suggest theologically?
adults.option_a: The north is associated with God's dwelling place in Israelite tradition (Isa 14:13, Ps 48:2); by omitting north, the psalmist implies promotion/judgment comes from God who dwells in the north — the divine direction is excluded because God is the source
adults.option_b: The north was simply forgotten
adults.option_c: North was excluded because no one lived there
adults.option_d: It is a scribal error
adults.explanation: Psalm 75:6 — East, west, south listed; north (tsaphon) omitted. Ps 48:2 — "the sides of the north, the city of the great King." Tsaphon was the divine mountain. The omission points to God as the source.

adults.question: How does God's first-person speech in Psalm 75:2-5 function within an Asaph psalm?
adults.option_a: It creates a prophetic oracle embedded within worship — Asaph speaks as God's mouthpiece, reflecting the Levitical worship leader's dual role as musician and prophet (1 Chr 25:1-2)
adults.option_b: God's speech is a later editorial insertion
adults.option_c: Asaph imagines what God might say
adults.option_d: The first-person speech is Asaph himself
adults.explanation: God speaks directly in vv.2-5. 1 Chr 25:1 — David set apart Asaph to "prophesy with harps." Asaph's role was both musical and prophetic — the divine oracle within a psalm reflects the cultic prophet tradition.

adults.question: What does "I bear up the pillars" (Psalm 75:3) reveal about cosmic stability and divine justice?
adults.option_a: God's sustaining of cosmic order is an act of will, not mechanical necessity — the same God who upholds the earth's pillars also judges at His appointed time; cosmic stability and moral judgment flow from the same sovereign will
adults.option_b: The pillars are literal geological structures
adults.option_c: God struggles to hold up the earth
adults.option_d: It describes Atlas from Greek mythology
adults.explanation: Psalm 75:3 — The dissolving earth is social/moral chaos; God's pillars are cosmic and moral order. Col 1:17 — "by him all things consist." God upholds both physics and ethics.

adults.question: How does the horn imagery (Psalm 75:4-5, 10) connect to messianic hope in the Psalter?
adults.option_a: The horn represents power — wicked horns cut off, righteous horns exalted; this develops through the Psalter toward the messianic King (Ps 89:17,24; 132:17) and culminates in Mary's Magnificat (Luke 1:52,69)
adults.option_b: Horns are literal animal horns
adults.option_c: The imagery is unique to Psalm 75
adults.option_d: Horns represent musical instruments
adults.explanation: Psalm 75:10 → Ps 89:17 → Ps 132:17 → Luke 1:69 — "hath raised up an horn of salvation for us." The horn trajectory leads to Christ as the ultimate exalted horn.

adults.question: What is the literary structure of Psalm 75 and how does it create theological balance?
adults.option_a: It alternates between human praise (vv.1,9) and divine speech (vv.2-5), with the centre (vv.6-8) providing theological explanation — God alone judges; the chiastic structure frames sovereignty within worship
adults.option_b: The psalm has no discernible structure
adults.option_c: It follows chronological narrative
adults.option_d: The structure is identical to all psalms
adults.explanation: Structure: A — thanksgiving (v.1); B — God speaks (vv.2-5); C — Centre: God alone promotes/demotes (vv.6-7); B' — judgment cup (v.8); A' — praise (vv.9-10). The chiastic pattern centres on God as sole judge.

adults.question: How does Psalm 75's theology of divine timing ("When I shall receive the congregation," v.2) address delayed justice?
adults.option_a: Justice has an appointed divine schedule that does not conform to human urgency — the apparent delay is human perception, not negligence, anticipating 2 Peter 3:9 where God's patience is purposeful
adults.option_b: God is sometimes too late
adults.option_c: The timing is metaphorical
adults.option_d: God judges immediately without delay
adults.explanation: Psalm 75:2 — The moed (appointed time) is God's, not ours. Hab 2:3 — "it will surely come, it will not tarry." 2 Pet 3:9 — "The Lord is not slack concerning his promise."

adults.question: What does "the wine is red; it is full of mixture" (Psalm 75:8) reveal about divine judgment?
adults.option_a: The spiced wine indicates fully prepared, potent judgment — not diluted; God's justice is deliberate, thorough, and proportionally crafted, not impulsive
adults.option_b: The red wine is grape juice
adults.option_c: The mixture is water to dilute judgment
adults.option_d: A literal wine recipe
adults.explanation: Psalm 75:8 — mesekh = spiced wine for maximum potency. Isa 51:17 — "thou hast drunken the dregs of the cup of trembling." The dregs are the most concentrated — the wicked drain every drop.

kids.question: Where is God known and where is His name great?
kids.explanation: Psalm 76:1 — "In Judah is God known: his name is great in Israel."

kids.question: What did God break in Psalm 76:3?
kids.option_a: The arrows, shield, sword, and the battle
kids.option_b: The walls of the enemy city
kids.explanation: Psalm 76:3 — "There brake he the arrows of the bow, the shield, and the sword, and the battle."

kids.question: What happened to the horse and chariot riders when God rebuked them?
kids.option_a: They fell into a dead sleep
kids.explanation: Psalm 76:6 — "At thy rebuke, O God of Jacob, both the chariot and horse are cast into a dead sleep."

teens.question: Where are God's tabernacle and dwelling place according to Psalm 76:2?
teens.option_a: In Salem (Jerusalem) and in Zion
teens.explanation: Psalm 76:2 — "In Salem also is his tabernacle, and his dwelling place in Zion."

teens.question: What happens when God arises to judgment in Psalm 76:8-9?
teens.option_a: The earth feared and was still — God arose to save the meek of the earth
teens.option_d: All people ran to the temple
teens.explanation: Psalm 76:8-9 — "the earth feared, and was still, When God arose to judgment, to save all the meek of the earth."

teens.question: What does Psalm 76:10 say about the wrath of man?
teens.option_a: The wrath of man shall praise God — He restrains the remainder of wrath
teens.option_b: Human anger always leads to good
teens.explanation: Psalm 76:10 — "Surely the wrath of man shall praise thee: the remainder of wrath shalt thou restrain."

teens.question: What should people do because God is to be feared (Psalm 76:11)?
teens.option_a: Vow and pay vows unto the LORD — bring presents to Him who ought to be feared
teens.explanation: Psalm 76:11 — "Vow, and pay unto the LORD your God: let all that be round about him bring presents unto him that ought to be feared."

teens.question: What does Psalm 76:12 say God does to rulers?
teens.option_a: He cuts off the spirit of princes and is terrible to kings of the earth
teens.explanation: Psalm 76:12 — "He shall cut off the spirit of princes: he is terrible to the kings of the earth."

adults.question: What is the significance of "Salem" as God's dwelling in Psalm 76:2?
adults.option_a: Salem connects Zion to Melchizedek's pre-Davidic city (Gen 14:18) — the name means "peace," establishing Jerusalem as the place of God's shalom with universal theological significance beyond tribal boundaries
adults.option_b: Salem is a different city from Jerusalem
adults.option_c: The name has no etymological significance
adults.option_d: It refers to a heavenly city
adults.explanation: Psalm 76:2 — Gen 14:18 — "Melchizedek king of Salem." Heb 7:2 — "King of Salem, which is, King of peace." The Melchizedek-Salem connection makes Zion's priesthood older than Levi.

adults.question: How does "the wrath of man shall praise thee" (Psalm 76:10) articulate divine sovereignty over evil?
adults.option_a: Even hostile human actions ultimately serve God's purposes — wrath intended to destroy becomes the occasion for glory; human evil is repurposed within divine governance, as in Joseph (Gen 50:20) and the cross (Acts 2:23)
adults.option_b: It means angry worship is acceptable
adults.option_c: Human wrath is always praiseworthy
adults.option_d: God causes human wrath
adults.explanation: Gen 50:20 — "ye thought evil...but God meant it unto good." Acts 2:23 — "by wicked hands have crucified and slain" yet "by the determinate counsel of God." Human evil is real, culpable, and yet sovereignly governed.

adults.question: What does the "dead sleep" of horse and chariot (Psalm 76:6) evoke in Israel's history?
adults.option_a: The Red Sea victory where Egypt's chariots were rendered powerless by divine intervention — radam (supernatural stupor) suggests divine causation; the psalm uses exodus language to celebrate God's ongoing power against military might
adults.option_b: It describes a literal sleeping spell on animals
adults.option_c: Normal nighttime rest
adults.option_d: A metaphor for diplomatic negotiations
adults.explanation: Psalm 76:6 — radam (deep sleep). The chariot-and-horse motif: Ex 15:1 — "the horse and his rider hath he thrown into the sea." Every military victory is re-enactment of the exodus.

adults.question: How does Psalm 76's divine warrior-judge contribute to Book III's theology of transcendence?
adults.option_a: God is supremely powerful — more glorious than mountains of prey, silencing the earth, cutting off princes; this transcendence serves Book III's argument that the God who seems absent from Israel's crisis is actually sovereign over all earthly power
adults.option_b: The warrior imagery contradicts God's peaceful nature
adults.option_c: Book III has no transcendence theology
adults.option_d: God is portrayed as one king among many
adults.explanation: Psalm 76: more glorious than mountains (v.4), silences earth (v.8), governs wrath (v.10), terrifies kings (v.12). Within Book III's crisis context, this transcendence insists: the God who allows crisis is not weak.

adults.question: What is the theological function of "the earth feared, and was still" (Psalm 76:8)?
adults.option_a: The still earth functions as the silenced courtroom when the Judge arises — creation becomes hushed witness to divine verdict; God's judgment is universal, affecting all creation and silencing all protest
adults.option_b: The earth literally stopped rotating
adults.option_c: It describes an earthquake
adults.option_d: Nothing unusual happened
adults.explanation: Cf. Hab 2:20 — "let all the earth keep silence before him." Zech 2:13 — "Be silent, O all flesh, before the LORD." Cosmic silence before divine judgment is a recurring motif: creation yields to the Judge.

adults.question: What specific historical event may Psalm 76 celebrate?
adults.option_a: Sennacherib's defeat — 2 Kings 19:35 describes the angel of the LORD smiting 185,000 Assyrians in their sleep, paralleling "the stouthearted are spoiled, they have slept their sleep" (v.5)
adults.option_b: It was composed for royal banquets
adults.option_c: It had no historical referent
adults.option_d: It was a private meditation
adults.explanation: Psalm 76:5 — "the stouthearted are spoiled, they have slept their sleep." 2 Kings 19:35 — "when they arose early in the morning, behold, they were all dead corpses." The parallels suggest Sennacherib's miraculous defeat.

adults.question: What does "bring presents unto him that ought to be feared" (Psalm 76:11) reveal about fear and worship?
adults.option_a: Fear of God is not the opposite of worship but its prerequisite — the God who terrifies kings is the God to whom offerings are brought; true worship is impossible without proper fear, and proper fear naturally produces worship
adults.option_b: Fear and worship are opposites
adults.option_c: Bringing presents is bribery
adults.option_d: The command applies only to foreigners
adults.explanation: Psalm 76:11-12 — Worship because He is feared. Prov 1:7 — "The fear of the LORD is the beginning of knowledge." Heb 12:28 — "serve God acceptably with reverence and godly fear." Biblical fear draws near, not away.

adults.question: How does Psalm 76's theology of saving "all the meek of the earth" (v.9) contribute to the biblical theme of divine preference for the humble?
adults.option_a: God's judicial intervention specifically targets the rescue of the meek (anavim) — divine power is exercised preferentially toward the powerless; this runs through the Psalter and peaks in the Beatitudes (Matt 5:5)
adults.option_b: God saves all people equally
adults.option_c: The meek is a specific tribe
adults.option_d: Saving the meek is secondary to demonstrating power
adults.explanation: Ps 37:11 — "the meek shall inherit the earth." Ps 147:6 — "The LORD lifteth up the meek." Matt 5:5 — "Blessed are the meek." The divine preferential option for the meek runs from Psalms to Beatitudes.

kids.question: What did Asaph do all night when he was sad?
kids.option_a: He stretched out his hand to God and his sore ran in the night
kids.option_c: He played music for the king
kids.explanation: Psalm 77:2 — "In the day of my trouble I sought the Lord: my sore ran in the night, and ceased not."

kids.question: What did Asaph remember to make himself feel better?
kids.option_a: God's wonders and works from long ago
kids.option_d: Songs his mother sang to him
kids.explanation: Psalm 77:11 — "I will remember the works of the LORD: surely I will remember thy wonders of old."

kids.question: What great event does Asaph recall God doing to the waters?
kids.option_a: The waters saw God and were afraid — He led His people through the sea
kids.option_b: He made the waters turn sweet
kids.option_c: He filled the waters with fish
kids.option_d: He made the waters stand still forever
kids.explanation: Psalm 77:16,20 — "The waters saw thee, O God, the waters saw thee; they were afraid...Thou leddest thy people like a flock."

teens.question: What anguished questions does Asaph ask in Psalm 77:7-9?
teens.option_a: "Will the Lord cast off for ever? Is his mercy clean gone? Hath God forgotten to be gracious?"
teens.option_b: "When will God send rain for the crops?"
teens.option_c: "Why do the wicked live so long?"
teens.option_d: "How many stars are in the sky?"
